Passing Handles as Variables and Parameters

The handle data type makes it possible to assign handles to parameters and variables (local, component, and global), as well as to non-database fields.

For example:

variables 
     handle vHandle 1
endvariables 
newinstance "testService", vHandle 2
vHandle->runTest("test1") 3

1 Declare a variable of type handle.

2 Create a new component instance and assign the returned handle to the variable.

3 Activate the runtest operation on the referenced object.

For more information, see handle.

Scope of a Handle Variable

A variable (or parameter) exists only as long as the trigger or operation in which it is declared is executing. When the trigger or operation ends, the variable (and the handle it contains) is deleted. If the variable was used in creating a new component instance, the component instance is also deleted.

If this is undesirable, use a component variable rather than a local variable to hold the handle.

Passing Handles Between Components

Handles can be passed between components as parameters to component, collection, and occurrence operations.

To be available for activation by external components, operations must be public and available in the component signature. Operations are public by default, but only public component operations are automatically included in component signature.

To include collection or occurrence operations in the component signature:

  1. In the model, declare at least one public operation for the entity in the appropriate entity trigger—Collection Operations or Occurrence Operations. For example: 
    public operation setColor
params
 string pColor : IN
endparams
; do something
return
end
    Note: You need to include the entity in the model even if it is a non-database entity.
  2. Compile the entity operations. Choose Edit > Compile Entity Operations.

Public and Partner Handles

Like operations, handles can be modified as public or partner. Public handles can only access public operations; partner handles can access both public and partner operations.

By default, a variable (or parameter) declaration with data type handle is public.

The ProcScript functions $instancehandle, $occhandle, and $collhandle each return a partner handle. However, partner handles are implicitly converted to public handles once they are exposed to other components. Public handles cannot be converted to partner handles.

Related Links
  • Activating Operations Using Handles

    After obtaining the handle of an object, you can activate the object's exposed operations using the handle operator (->). The return value of an operation that has been activated using a handle is returned inline.

  • Public and Partner Operations

    Operations can be modified using the public or partner modifier. Public operations can be invoked from other components. Partner operations can only be invoked from the component in which they are defined.
